course_description:
This course provides students with the opportunity to complete an independent research thesis in economics over a single semester. Working under the guidance of an academic supervisor, students will identify a research question, situate it within the relevant literature, apply appropriate methods of analysis, and present their findings in a clear and scholarly manner. In the course of their research, students are expected to review and critically evaluate the state of the art in their chosen area, produce a structured literature review, engage in regular discussions with their supervisor, present results in a cohesive and rigorous way, assess the robustness of their findings, and identify the limitations of their work to motivate future research. The thesis represents the culmination of the Master's program and provides students with the experience of producing a substantial piece of original research to a standard that could form the basis of a publishable contribution. Students wishing to enrol must first secure supervision from an academic staff member and agree on a research topic. A thesis enrolment form, outlining the proposed research and supervisor approval, must be submitted prior to enrolment. Enrolments will open in November of the preceding year. Further details can be found here .

This course enables students to demonstrate their ability to conduct independent research under the guidance of an academic supervisor. Students wishing to enrol must first secure supervision from an academic staff member and agree on a research topic. Students must also have an approved Research Thesis Application before enrolment.

Students will conduct scholarly research and produce an approximately 15,000- to 20,000-word thesis that meets high academic standards . The course promotes critical thinking and informed judgment , allowing students to apply economic theory and analysis from their postgraduate Master’s program .

course_contacts.name: School Enquiries, School of Economics
course_contacts.notes: All enquiries regarding student and academic administration (i.e. non-course content information, e.g., class allocation, timetables, extension to assessment due date, etc.) should be directed to enquiries@economics.uq.edu.au . Enquiries relating specifically to course content should be directed to the Course Coordinator/Lecturer.ﾠ

timetable:
Additional timetable information
Lectures
Since students are expected to conduct independent research, there will only be one (1) lecture at the beginning of the semester. Lecture details (date, time, and venue) will be available on Blackboard.
Finding a Thesis Supervisor
Students must secure supervision from an academic staff member and agree on a research topic before enrolment in this course.
It is students' responsibility to find their supervisors. Supervisors will NOT be assigned to students.
Check the
research areas and staff members
in the School of Economics. Get in touch with a
potential supervisor
and develop your research topic in collaboration with the supervisor.
You can reach out to potential supervisor to discuss possible research topics as early as possible -- there is no need to wait until you are about to enrol in this course. Indeed, not all academic staff are available for supervision all the time, so
early consultation is essential.
Once you have an agreement from a supervisor and a defined research topic in your chosen research area,
submit your Research Thesis Application online by the beginning of Orientation Week.
See
application instructions and submission link here
.
Regular Meetings with Supervisor(s)
Students should meet regularly -- ideally weekly but at least fortnightly -- with their supervisor(s) at a mutually agreed time to discuss their thesis progress.
Any major changes or developments in the research plan should be considered jointly with your supervisor(s) before implementation.
If appropriate advisory support is not forthcoming from your supervisor(s), contact the Thesis Coordinator (see Course Enquiries) as soon as the problem emerges.
Progression Checkpoints
Beginning of Orientation Week
(16 February 2026):
Research Thesis Application
due online.
Beginning of Week 9
(27 April 2026): Thesis Abstract Form (available on Blackboard) due. Submit via the Thesis Abstract Form submission link on Blackboard. Information in this abstract will facilitate examiners selection. You can revise your abstract afterwards.
course_aims: The purposes of the Thesis are:

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate that you can conduct independent research work.
Write up your research undertaken at a high academic standard.
Think critically and make informed judgement.
Communicate what you do in the written form.
Achieve a comprehensive and in-depth knowledge in your field.

assessment_details.title: Thesis Submission
assessment_details.task_description: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Research Approach Your thesis may be empirical or theoretical. However, bear in mind that theoretical research is often more challenging. A strong thesis should exhibit a clear thematic structure. The introduction must clearly outline the research objectives and the techniques employed. It may also be beneficial to include a brief literature review on directly relevant topics. Arguments should be presented systematically in a logical and coherent manner. Each chapter should begin with an explanation of its role within the thesis to guide the reader effectively. The progression of the thesis should be structured to lead as directly as possible to a logical conclusion. Assessment will be based not only on the discussion and conclusions but also on the research process, the strength of the argument, and the overall presentation quality. Thesis Structure Every thesis must include the following components in addition to the main research exposition: Introduction: Includes background information, research objectives, significance of the study, research questions and/or hypotheses, and an outline of the thesis structure. Theoretical Framework: Discusses the relevant economic theories underpinning the research. If no established economic theory applies, this section should justify why economic expertise is necessary and relevant. Literature Review: Critically examines recent economic and related literature. This section should be more focused than a broader literature outline in the Introduction. Data Analysis (for an empirical thesis): Describes data sources, potential limitations, and the analytical techniques employed. OR Theoretical Analysis (for a theoretical thesis): Describes the theoretical model, analysis, and proofs. Results: Presents findings from the analysis. Discussion and Conclusion: Analyzes the implications and limitations of the research. The conclusion should not merely summarize previous chapters but should include policy recommendations (if applicable) and suggestions for future research. Language and Writing Standards A high standard of English is expected. You are encouraged to consult academic writing resources. Your supervisor can offer guidance but will not correct poor writing. Students who wish to seek help on language and writing skills are strongly encouraged to consult UQ Student Services First Steps in Research (for thesis writing) or Assignment Writing (for academic writing skills). Draft Submission You should submit a draft to your advisor at least two weeks before the due date to receive feedback. It is advisable to submit individual chapters progressively throughout the semester. Last-minute submissions often result in avoidable errors and may impact grading. Length The thesis should be 15,000 to 20,000 words , including footnotes but excluding tables, figures, and references. Formatting Guidelines Paper Size: A4 (297 x 210 mm) Font: Preferably 12-point (minimum 11-point for body text; smaller fonts acceptable in tables/figures) Line Spacing: Double spacing (1.5 spacing is allowed) Margins: Left: 3cm; Right: 2cm; Top and Bottom: 2cm Pagination: Consecutive page numbering Submission: Electronic submission via Turnitin on Blackboard (no hard copies required) Required Thesis Sections Title Page The University of Queensland Faculty of Business, Economics and Law School of Economics Thesis title (in full) Course name and degree program Student's name and previous degrees Acknowledgements Declaration Statement (Signed copy required) Confrims originality of work and specifies thesis length Abstract (Max: 200 words) Outlines the research aims, scope, conclusions, and key terms (up to six) Table of Contents Lists of Figures, Tables, Illustrations, and Diagrams (as appropriate) Acronyms and Abbreviations Main Thesis Chapters References (APA 7th style) Appendices (if applicable) Referencing Style APA 7th (see UQ Library's guide ) Footnotes, Tables, and Figures Footnotes should be used sparingly and are included in the word count. Tables, figures, and references are excluded from the word count. Large tables should be placed in an appendix. Academic Integrity Academic integrity and appropriate conduct is non-negotiable. Unacceptable behaviours include, but are not limited to Plagiarism; Fabrication of references or evidence; Failure to reference Artificial Intelligence (AI) use; and Soliciting or aiding and abetting contract cheating. Academic misconduct will be handled in accordance with UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy . Accidental plagiarism is still considered plagiarism and will be handled as an academic misconduct. See UQ Library's Guide on Plagiarism if you are unsure what requires referencing. Referencing and Acknowledging AI or MT use Reference AI and MT use using APA 7th style in the references of your written proposal. AI use should then be acknowledged using a table like the Example Cover Sheet provided by UQ Library (expand "Example Cover Sheet" under "How to acknowledge your use of AI in assessment"). The table should be inserted after the references. Using Previous Research If your thesis builds upon your ECON7950 Research Proposal , you must clearly reference specific sections rather than copying large portions of text. Citation format: (Your surname, year, p. ?) e.g., (Anthony, 2018, p. 3) Example reference entry: Anthony, J. (2019). Food security in India: Achievements and challenges. Research proposal submitted for ECON7950 (Research Methods in Economics). Submission and Assessment Only electronic submissions via Turnitin on Blackboard are accepted. The marking criteria can be accessed via Learn.UQ or mySI-net. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
ONLY an electronic copy must be submitted via the Turnitin link on the course Blackboard site by the due date.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Note that computer problems are not acceptable reasons for requesting an extension.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension .
assessment_details.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .
learning_resources.seeking_advice: In addition to regular meetings with your thesis supervisor, you are also encouraged to seek advice from other academic staff with expertise in relevant areas.
learning_resources.first_steps_in_research_program: First Steps in Research (FSR) is an Honours/Masters thesis writing support program that comprises four online sessions across each semester. The program is recommended for all those students engaged in their first major research project and thesis across all disciplines at UQ. See UQ Student Services First Steps in Research page for information and registration.
learning_resources.uq_library_resources: Accessing previous thesis: Printed theses (pre-2011) can be found in the Central Library. Post-2011 theses are available in electronic format via the UQ Library e-space . UQ Library's APA 7th guide : This course requires APA 7th citation style UQ Library Acknowledge and Reference AI use guide : Please use the Example Cover Sheet format in this guide to acknowledge your AI use UQ Library's EndNote guide : How to use EndNote to store, organise and create references in your documents Contact your Faculty Services Librarian : For library research support (e.g., assistance with the various datasets available at UQ)
learning_resources.uq_student_services_resources: Assignment writing support : Academic writing, writing literature review, find a proofreader Time management and procrastination Health and well-being support
learning_resources.funding_for_additional_resources: The School of Economics cannot guarantee any funding for master's thesis projects. You should discuss with your supervisor if your thesis project may require funding.

learning_activities.topic: Meeting with Coordinator Discussion regarding requirements of thesis, guidelines for the presentation of thesis, milestones due, and resources available. Learning outcomes: L01

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 2 To Week 13 (02 Mar - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Research and Write thesis Conduct research, undertake analysis and write thesis, regularly meeting your supervisor(s). Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library .
